affected_groups: Roma students affected by segregated schooling
content.decision_question: Should the state extend admissions or anti-discrimination requirements to reach maintainer-exempt schools even where this conflicts with established institutional autonomy protections, or should reform efforts be confined to the public-sector segment already under ministerial authority, accepting that a segregation-producing sector will remain outside reach?
content.evidence_boundary: Legal analysis can clarify the scope of existing exemptions and the likely judicial reception of a review effort, and further data can quantify how much segregation is attributable to the exempt sector versus the public sector, but no empirical evidence can resolve the underlying question of how much weight institutional/religious autonomy should carry against equal-access claims — this is a constitutional and normative value conflict, not an empirical one.
content.tension: Several proposals (T2, T3, T5) rely on Klebelsberg Centre or ministerial authority, but church-maintained and private schools sit outside this authority and can resist, opt out, or absorb displaced selective demand, while T4's legal-exemption-review pathway directly confronts a judicial precedent (2015 Curia ruling) protecting religious-institution discretion. Expanding state authority over admissions or exemptions could close equity gaps but directly threatens constitutionally and historically protected maintainer autonomy that was foundational to the 1989-90 settlement.
content.title: Central authority reach versus maintainer and institutional autonomy
